course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: Which individual is most closely associated with the mechanised revolution of the 18th and 19th-century agrarian period that laid early foundations for computing sciences?
option_a: Charles Babbage
option_b: Thomas Edison
option_c: Alan Turing
option_d: Henry Ford
rationale: Charles Babbage is explicitly identified in the course materials as the key figure whose work influenced the first stage (Agrarian Period) of industrial revolution, during which mechanised computation concepts were developed.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: Which era of industrial development introduced electricity and mass production, marking the transition from purely mechanical systems to electrically powered industrial processes?
option_a: The Global Tech Era
option_b: The Manual Labour Period
option_c: The Technological Advancement Period
option_d: The Agrarian Period
rationale: The Manual Labour Period is defined as the 19th and 20th-century era that introduced electricity and mass production, distinguishing it from the earlier mechanised agrarian period and the later digital era.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: A student argues that the invention of the personal computer belongs to the Manual Labour Period because it involved electrical engineering. Which statement best explains why this classification is incorrect?
option_a: Personal computers were invented before electricity was discovered
option_b: The Manual Labour Period did not involve any technological innovation
option_c: Personal computers emerged in the Technological Advancement Period, which is characterised by digital computing and microprocessors rather than just electricity
option_d: Personal computers are classified under the Global Tech Era because they use artificial intelligence
rationale: The Technological Advancement Period is specifically characterised by digital computing, microprocessors, and personal computers, distinguishing it from the earlier Manual Labour Period which was defined by electricity and mass production rather than digital technology.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: Which sequence correctly orders the four stages of industrial revolution from earliest to most recent?
option_a: Global Tech Era → Technological Advancement Period → Manual Labour Period → Agrarian Period
option_b: Manual Labour Period → Agrarian Period → Technological Advancement Period → Global Tech Era
option_c: Technological Advancement Period → Manual Labour Period → Agrarian Period → Global Tech Era
option_d: Agrarian Period → Manual Labour Period → Technological Advancement Period → Global Tech Era
rationale: The correct chronological progression begins with the 18th-19th century Agrarian Period (mechanisation), followed by the 19th-20th century Manual Labour Period (electricity and mass production), then the mid-20th century Technological Advancement Period (digital computing and microprocessors), and finally the contemporary Global Tech Era (artificial intelligence).

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: A historian is documenting how online education platforms became viable during the COVID-19 pandemic. Under which stage of industrial revolution would she most appropriately classify the technological infrastructure that enabled global online classes?
option_a: The Global Tech Era, because it relies on AI-driven networks and global connectivity that characterise this fourth stage
option_b: The Technological Advancement Period, because personal computers alone enabled online learning
option_c: The Manual Labour Period, because electricity powers the servers hosting online platforms
option_d: The Agrarian Period, because mechanisation laid the groundwork for all subsequent communication
rationale: Online classes exemplify the Global Tech Era (fourth stage), which the course materials explicitly cite as the era bringing about global productivity through interconnected technologies such as online education platforms.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: A manufacturing company replaces its assembly line workers with robotic arms controlled by AI systems. This transformation is most directly characteristic of which transition between industrial stages?
option_a: From the Agrarian Period to the Manual Labour Period
option_b: From the Technological Advancement Period to the Global Tech Era
option_c: From the Manual Labour Period to the Technological Advancement Period
option_d: From the Global Tech Era back to the Technological Advancement Period
rationale: The replacement of human labour with AI-controlled robotics represents the defining shift of the Global Tech Era (fourth stage), in which artificial intelligence and automation fundamentally transform workforce structures.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: An economist notes that while global productivity has increased dramatically since 2010, traditional manufacturing jobs have declined sharply in developed nations. Which stage of industrial revolution best explains this dual phenomenon?
option_a: The Agrarian Period, because mechanisation eliminated farming jobs
option_b: The Manual Labour Period, because mass production created factory jobs
option_c: The Global Tech Era, because globalisation and AI have increased productivity while displacing traditional human roles
option_d: The Technological Advancement Period, because personal computers replaced all manual labour
rationale: The course materials identify the Global Tech Era as the period in which globalisation has led to increased global productivity while simultaneously transforming the workforce through robotics and AI taking over traditional human tasks.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: Which comparison most accurately distinguishes the Technological Advancement Period from the Global Tech Era?
option_a: The Technological Advancement Period focused on analogue systems while the Global Tech Era uses digital systems
option_b: The Technological Advancement Period was limited to developed nations while the Global Tech Era is worldwide
option_c: The Technological Advancement Period introduced electricity while the Global Tech Era introduced the internet
option_d: The Technological Advancement Period brought digital computing and personal computers, while the Global Tech Era introduced artificial intelligence that autonomously transforms industries
rationale: The key distinction lies in the nature of technological capability: the Technological Advancement Period is defined by digital computing characterised by microprocessors and personal computers, whereas the Global Tech Era is distinguished by artificial intelligence that fundamentally alters how work is performed.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: A policy researcher claims that the physical constraints of factory production in the 1920s are fundamentally different from the resource constraints of AI-driven production today. Which analysis best supports this claim?
option_a: The 1920s Manual Labour Period was constrained by physical infrastructure and human labour capacity, while the Global Tech Era is constrained by data quality, algorithmic bias, and computational power rather than physical limitations
option_b: There is no meaningful difference because both periods rely on electricity
option_c: The 1920s had fewer constraints because factories could operate without software
option_d: Modern AI production has no constraints because digital systems are infinitely scalable
rationale: The course materials contrast the physical constraints of the manual labour period with the automated, knowledge-driven nature of the global tech era, highlighting how the shift from physical to digital production changes the nature of limiting factors.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
concept: The Four Stages of Industrial Revolution
stem: A government proposes to slow down AI adoption to protect traditional jobs. Using the framework of the four stages of industrial revolution, which evaluation best assesses this proposal?
option_a: This is justified because the Agrarian Period proved that technological change should always be resisted
option_b: This is unlikely to succeed historically because each industrial stage has inevitably transformed the workforce, suggesting that adaptation strategies would be more effective than resistance
option_c: This is unnecessary because the Global Tech Era will not affect employment
option_d: This is the only effective approach because previous industrial revolutions did not displace workers
rationale: The framework of industrial stages demonstrates that technological transformation of the workforce is a consistent historical pattern across all four stages, making adaptation and retraining strategies more historically grounded than attempts to resist technological progression.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: What is the most accurate definition of the digital divide?
option_a: The gap between people who understand programming and those who do not
option_b: The difference in internet speed between urban and rural areas
option_c: The gap between those who have access to modern computing technologies and those who do not
option_d: The separation between computer science and information systems as academic fields
rationale: The digital divide is explicitly defined as the gap between those who have access to modern computing technologies and those who do not, noting that this disparity exists both within and between countries.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A journalist reports that in certain developing regions, fewer than 30% of households have reliable internet access, while in developed nations the figure exceeds 90%. Which concept best describes this disparity?
option_a: The Digital Skills Gap
option_b: Workforce Transformation
option_c: Computing Discipline Inequality
option_d: The Digital Divide
rationale: This scenario exemplifies the digital divide — the gap between those who have access to modern computing technologies and those who do not — which the course materials state exists both within and between countries.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which statement accurately distinguishes the "digital divide" from the "digital skills gap"?
option_a: The digital divide concerns access to technology, while the digital skills gap concerns the ability to use technology effectively
option_b: The digital divide affects only developing countries, while the digital skills gap affects only developed countries
option_c: The digital divide refers to internet speed, while the digital skills gap refers to computer literacy
option_d: They are different names for the same phenomenon
rationale: The course materials explicitly contrast the digital divide (access to technology) with the digital skills gap (competence in using technology), noting that the two are frequently confused despite addressing different dimensions of digital inequality.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A non-profit organisation distributes free laptops to low-income students in rural communities. Which type of inequality is this programme most directly addressing?
option_a: The Digital Skills Gap
option_b: The Digital Divide
option_c: Workforce Displacement
option_d: Hardware Obsolescence
rationale: By providing physical computing devices to those who lack them, the programme directly targets the digital divide — the gap in access to modern computing technologies — rather than the skills gap which relates to usage competence.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: During the COVID-19 pandemic, a nation with high ICT penetration successfully transitioned to remote work and online education, while a neighbouring nation with limited ICT infrastructure suffered severe economic and educational disruption. Which concept best explains this divergence in outcomes?
option_a: E-waste accumulation
option_b: Computing discipline specialisation
option_c: The Digital Divide
option_d: Hardware obsolescence cycles
rationale: The divergence in outcomes directly illustrates the digital divide: the disparity in access to Information and Communication Technology (ICT) between nations creates unequal capacity to adapt to digital-dependent crises.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why the digital divide reinforces existing class structures within a society?
option_a: Wealthier individuals simply purchase more expensive devices than poorer individuals
option_b: Digital technologies have no impact on social mobility
option_c: Government policies intentionally restrict technology access to lower classes
option_d: Those without ICT access cannot participate fully in the digital economy, limiting their educational and economic opportunities and thereby perpetuating social stratification
rationale: The course materials state that the disparity in technology access creates economic and social inequalities that effectively reinforce existing class structures, as those without access are excluded from digital economic and educational opportunities.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A researcher observes that within a single city, affluent neighbourhoods have fibre-optic internet while low-income neighbourhoods rely on outdated infrastructure. Which analysis best characterises this situation?
option_a: The digital divide can exist within countries and communities, not just between nations
option_b: This demonstrates that the digital divide only affects urban areas
option_c: This proves that the digital divide has been resolved in developed nations
option_d: This situation reflects the digital skills gap rather than the digital divide
rationale: The course materials explicitly state that the digital divide exists both within and between countries, making this intra-city disparity a clear example of the phenomenon rather than evidence of its resolution.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: An international agency proposes that donating second-hand computers to developing nations will eliminate the digital divide within five years. Which response best applies course concepts to this proposal?
option_a: This will completely solve the problem because hardware access is the only component of the digital divide
option_b: This is unnecessary because the digital divide only affects a small number of people globally
option_c: This approach is harmful because second-hand computers always create e-waste problems
option_d: This is insufficient because the digital divide encompasses infrastructure, connectivity, and maintenance capabilities, meaning hardware alone cannot bridge the gap without systemic investment
rationale: The digital divide is a multi-dimensional gap involving access to modern computing technologies broadly, not merely possession of hardware. Effective intervention requires addressing infrastructure, connectivity, skills, and sustained support rather than one-time hardware donations.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A tech company argues that the digital divide will naturally disappear as smartphones become cheaper worldwide. Which evaluation best assesses this claim?
option_a: This is certain because smartphone ownership is increasing in all countries
option_b: This is impossible because technology always becomes more expensive over time
option_c: This is overly optimistic because access to low-cost devices does not guarantee reliable connectivity, digital literacy, or equitable use of online services
option_d: This has already happened, so the digital divide no longer exists
rationale: The digital divide encompasses more than device ownership — it includes quality of access, connectivity infrastructure, and the capacity to use technology meaningfully. Cheaper devices alone do not address these broader structural inequalities.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A government mandates that all citizens must complete tax filings online. Which evaluation best assesses the equity implications of this policy?
option_a: This is equitable because everyone has equal access to the internet
option_b: This is the most efficient approach and therefore cannot disadvantage anyone
option_c: This policy has no relation to the digital divide
option_d: This may disproportionately disadvantage citizens who lack reliable ICT access or digital skills, potentially reinforcing the social inequalities associated with the digital divide
rationale: Mandating online-only services assumes universal ICT access and competence, which directly contradicts the reality of the digital divide. Such policies can exclude those without access or skills, reinforcing the economic and social inequalities the course materials identify.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which computing discipline focuses specifically on protecting data, systems, and networks from cyber attacks?
option_a: Data Science and Artificial Intelligence
option_b: Cyber Security
option_c: Information System
option_d: Software Engineering
rationale: Cyber Security is explicitly defined as the computing discipline that focuses on protecting data, systems, and networks from cyber attacks, distinguishing it from the other fields listed.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A university student designs algorithms to optimise route planning for delivery vehicles. Which computing discipline best describes this work?
option_a: Computer Science, because it involves theoretical foundations of computing algorithms
option_b: Information Systems, because it bridges computing and business operations
option_c: Software Engineering, because it involves software development
option_d: Cyber Security, because route data must be protected
rationale: Computer Science focuses on the theoretical foundations of computing algorithms and software development. Designing optimisation algorithms for route planning is fundamentally a computer science activity because it centres on algorithmic theory rather than business application or software engineering processes.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which statement best distinguishes Computer Science from Information Systems?
option_a: Computer Science is a subset of Information Systems
option_b: Computer Science focuses on theoretical algorithms, while Information Systems bridges computing and business operations to optimise efficiency
option_c: Information Systems does not involve any computing knowledge
option_d: They are identical fields with different names
rationale: The course materials explicitly contrast Computer Science (theoretical algorithms) with Information Systems (business operations application), noting that students frequently conflate the two despite their distinct focuses.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A hospital wants to analyse patient records to predict disease outbreaks in its region. Which computing discipline would lead this initiative?
option_a: Cyber Security
option_b: Software Engineering
option_c: Data Science and Artificial Intelligence
option_d: Information Systems
rationale: Data Science and Artificial Intelligence is defined as the discipline that analyses large datasets to extract insights and make predictions. Predicting disease outbreaks from patient records is a data science task involving pattern recognition and predictive modelling.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A retail company needs a system that integrates its inventory, sales, and customer data into a single platform to improve operational efficiency. Which computing discipline is most relevant to this need?
option_a: Cyber Security
option_b: Computer Science
option_c: Data Science and Artificial Intelligence
option_d: Information Systems
rationale: Information Systems bridges the gap between computing and business operations to optimise efficiency. Integrating inventory, sales, and customer data for operational improvement is the defining application of information systems.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A technology firm is developing a new mobile banking application. The project requires secure transaction protocols, an intuitive user interface, and compliance with financial regulations. Which analysis best explains why multiple computing disciplines must collaborate on this project?
option_a: Cyber Security handles transaction protection, Software Engineering manages the design and development lifecycle, and Information Systems ensures regulatory compliance and business integration
option_b: Only Software Engineering is needed because coding is the only requirement
option_c: Only Cyber Security matters because banking applications face hacking threats
option_d: Computer Science alone can address all aspects because algorithms solve every problem
rationale: The course materials identify multiple computing disciplines that each address distinct aspects of technology projects. Complex real-world applications like mobile banking inherently require interdisciplinary collaboration across security, engineering, and business systems domains.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why government policies on workforce retraining are necessary in the computing field?
option_a: Computing disciplines are becoming obsolete and workers need to leave the field
option_b: Computing disciplines create new opportunities while displacing traditional roles, requiring policies to help workers transition to emerging positions
option_c: All computing jobs require the same skills, so retraining is unnecessary
option_d: Workforce retraining is only needed in developing countries
rationale: The course materials state that computing disciplines influence job markets by creating new opportunities while also displacing traditional roles, necessitating government policies on workforce retraining and ethical governance.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A university proposes merging its Computer Science and Cyber Security departments into a single school to reduce administrative costs. Which evaluation best assesses this proposal?
option_a: This is ideal because the two disciplines are essentially the same
option_b: This will have no impact on the quality of either programme
option_c: This risks weakening both programmes because Computer Science focuses on theoretical algorithm foundations while Cyber Security specialises in data and network protection, requiring distinct expertise and curricula
option_d: This is the only financially responsible option for any university
rationale: Computer Science and Cyber Security, while related, have fundamentally different core focuses — theoretical algorithms versus protection of data and systems. Merging them risks diluting the specialised expertise each discipline requires.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A company wants to build a secure, scalable e-commerce platform from the ground up. Which computing discipline would be primarily responsible for the architecture and ongoing maintenance of this platform?
option_a: Data Science and Artificial Intelligence
option_b: Information Systems
option_c: Cyber Security
option_d: Software Engineering
rationale: Software Engineering involves designing, developing, and maintaining software systems. Building and maintaining a scalable e-commerce platform is the core responsibility of software engineering, though other disciplines would contribute specialised components.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A pharmaceutical company uses machine learning to identify potential drug compounds from molecular databases. Which computing discipline best describes this application?
option_a: Data Science and Artificial Intelligence
option_b: Software Engineering
option_c: Information Systems
option_d: Cyber Security
rationale: Analysing large datasets (molecular databases) to extract insights and make predictions (identifying drug compounds) is the defining function of Data Science and Artificial Intelligence as described in the course materials.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: What is the primary function of the Central Processing Unit (CPU) in a computer system?
option_a: Handling all tasks and coordinating communication between components
option_b: Storing data permanently for long-term access
option_c: Providing external connectivity to peripheral devices
option_d: Converting electrical signals into visual displays
rationale: The CPU is explicitly described as "the computer brain handling all tasks and coordinating communication" in the course materials, distinguishing it from memory, storage, and peripheral functions.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A student turns on her computer and the operating system loads immediately. Which hardware component makes this rapid startup possible by storing the essential instructions the computer needs to boot?
option_a: RAM, because it stores all active data temporarily
option_b: ROM, because it contains permanent instructions for startup loading that rarely change
option_c: The CPU, because it processes the boot commands
option_d: The motherboard, because it distributes power to all components
rationale: ROM (Read Only Memory) is defined as permanent storage for vital instructions that rarely change, including startup loading procedures. This distinguishes it from RAM, which stores data actively being used but does not retain boot instructions permanently.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A graphic designer is experiencing significant slowdown while working with large image files. Her IT consultant recommends upgrading a specific hardware component. Which component, if upgraded, would most directly improve her computer's ability to handle multiple large files simultaneously?
option_a: ROM
option_b: The motherboard
option_c: RAM, because it stores data actively being used and more capacity allows smoother handling of multiple large files
option_d: The CPU exclusively, because all performance depends on processor speed
rationale: RAM (Random Access Memory) is described as short-term memory storing data actively being used. When working with multiple large files, insufficient RAM causes the system to slow down as it cannot hold all active data in fast-access memory.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A technician is assembling a custom computer and needs to ensure all components can communicate with each other and receive adequate power. Which component serves as the central connection and power distribution hub?
option_a: The CPU
option_b: RAM modules
option_c: ROM chips
option_d: The motherboard
rationale: The motherboard is defined as the central electrical circuit that connects all different parts and distributes power, making it the foundational component for hardware integration.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A user accidentally spills water on her laptop keyboard. The laptop still powers on but the keyboard no longer responds. Which category of hardware component has been affected?
option_a: A peripheral input component, because the keyboard is an externally connected item that controls input functions
option_b: The CPU, because all input is processed there
option_c: ROM, because keyboard settings are stored there
option_d: The motherboard, because it has been directly damaged by the water
rationale: Computer hardware includes peripheral components that are externally connected to control either input or output functions. The keyboard is a peripheral input device, and the scenario describes damage limited to this external component while the core system still functions.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why a computer cannot function with hardware alone, without any software installed?
option_a: Hardware generates its own instructions automatically
option_b: Hardware consists of physical components that cannot execute tasks without software, which provides the manufactured set of instructions that tell the hardware what operations to perform
option_c: Modern hardware no longer requires software to operate
option_d: Software is merely an optional enhancement to hardware performance
rationale: The course materials explicitly state that hardware cannot function without software, defining software as a manufactured set of instructions that hardware needs to perform operations. This establishes the interdependent relationship between physical components and instructional code.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best distinguishes RAM from ROM in terms of their functional roles during computer operation?
option_a: RAM is larger in physical size than ROM
option_b: RAM stores data permanently while ROM stores data temporarily
option_c: RAM holds data actively being used that changes frequently, while ROM stores vital startup instructions that remain largely unchanged
option_d: ROM is faster than RAM because it contains essential instructions
rationale: RAM is defined as short-term memory storing data actively being used, while ROM is defined as permanent storage for vital instructions that rarely change (e.g., startup loading). Their functional distinction lies in volatility and purpose, not size or speed.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A consumer claims that purchasing the most expensive CPU available will guarantee the best computer performance for all tasks. Which evaluation best assesses this claim?
option_a: This is correct because the CPU determines all aspects of performance
option_b: This is incorrect because only RAM affects performance
option_c: This is correct for gaming but incorrect for business applications
option_d: This is oversimplified because optimal performance depends on balanced hardware components, including adequate RAM, compatible motherboard, and appropriate storage, not just CPU specification
rationale: The course materials describe the CPU as handling all tasks and coordinating communication, but optimal functioning requires understanding of all hardware components. Performance bottlenecks can occur in RAM, storage, or motherboard compatibility regardless of CPU quality.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A data centre manager needs to select hardware for a server that will run continuously for months without rebooting. Which consideration is most critical for this specific use case?
option_a: All core hardware components must be optimised for 24/7 operation, with particular attention to cooling systems and power supply stability that the motherboard must distribute effectively
option_b: Only the CPU matters because servers primarily process data
option_c: ROM capacity is the most important factor for continuous operation
option_d: Standard consumer-grade hardware is sufficient for any server application
rationale: The motherboard distributes power to all components, and the CPU coordinates communication. For continuous operation, all components must be enterprise-grade, with particular attention to power distribution stability and thermal management to prevent hardware failure during extended runtime.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains the relationship between hardware obsolescence and the social issues surrounding computer hardware?
option_a: Hardware obsolescence has no social implications
option_b: Rapid hardware obsolescence leads to frequent disposal of electronic devices, which raises ethical concerns about labour rights in manufacturing and environmental impacts from improper disposal
option_c: Social issues only affect software development, not hardware
option_d: Hardware obsolescence benefits society by creating more jobs in manufacturing
rationale: The course materials note that social sciences examine broader implications of hardware production, including labour rights violations in manufacturing and unequal access to devices. Hardware obsolescence directly contributes to these issues by driving the production and disposal cycle.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: What is the primary environmental hazard associated with improper disposal of electronic waste?
option_a: Increased noise pollution from discarded devices
option_b: Excessive consumption of landfill space
option_c: Toxic substances leaching from waste into soil and water
option_d: Radiation emissions from old computer monitors
rationale: The course materials identify toxic contamination — the leaching of toxic substances from e-waste into soil and water — as the key environmental hazard resulting from improper disposal of discarded electronic devices.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why rapid technological advancement makes e-waste management particularly challenging?
option_a: Technology advancement has no impact on waste generation
option_b: Newer devices are always biodegradable
option_c: People prefer to keep old devices indefinitely
option_d: Hardware obsolescence leads to rapid aging of devices and frequent disposal, continuously increasing the volume of e-waste that requires proper management
rationale: The course materials explicitly link hardware obsolescence — the rapid aging of hardware — to frequent waste generation, creating a continuous stream of discarded electronics that poses ongoing environmental management challenges.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A city council discovers that residents are discarding old smartphones and laptops in regular household bins. Which policy intervention would most directly address the environmental hazard identified in the course materials?
option_a: Establishing designated e-waste collection points and recycling programmes to prevent toxic substances from leaching into soil and water
option_b: Banning all smartphone and laptop sales in the city
option_c: Encouraging residents to burn electronic waste to reduce volume
option_d: Ignoring the issue because electronic waste is biodegradable
rationale: The primary environmental hazard of improper e-waste disposal is toxic contamination of soil and water. Designated collection and recycling programmes directly prevent this by ensuring proper processing of hazardous components rather than landfill disposal.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A technology company designs its new smartphones with easily replaceable batteries and modular components that can be upgraded individually. Which e-waste management principle does this design strategy best exemplify?
option_a: Disposal efficiency
option_b: Reduction of hardware obsolescence by extending device lifespan through modular upgrades rather than full device replacement
option_c: Increased consumption of raw materials
option_d: Planned obsolescence acceleration
rationale: Hardware obsolescence is identified as the rapid aging of hardware leading to frequent waste. Modular design directly counteracts this by allowing component upgrades rather than full device replacement, thereby reducing the rate at which entire devices become obsolete.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best contrasts the technical perspective on hardware with the sociological perspective as described in the course materials?
option_a: Both perspectives focus exclusively on device performance
option_b: The technical perspective examines user behaviour while the sociological perspective examines device specifications
option_c: The technical perspective focuses on hardware efficiency and performance, while the sociological perspective examines post-consumer environmental impacts and labour rights in manufacturing
option_d: There is no meaningful difference between the two perspectives
rationale: The course materials explicitly contrast the purely technical focus on hardware efficiency and performance with the sociological focus on post-consumer environmental impacts, highlighting how these two perspectives examine fundamentally different aspects of hardware lifecycles.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why e-waste management is described as an ethical responsibility in technology lifecycles?
option_a: E-waste management is only a legal requirement, not an ethical issue
option_b: Ethical concerns only apply to software, not hardware
option_c: Technology companies are not responsible for their products after sale
option_d: The improper disposal of e-waste creates environmental hazards and raises concerns about labour rights in manufacturing, making responsible management a moral obligation throughout the technology lifecycle
rationale: The course materials frame e-waste management as highlighting the ethical and environmental responsibilities required in technology lifecycles, connecting post-consumer disposal impacts with broader social justice concerns about production conditions.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: An environmental NGO is campaigning for legislation to hold electronics manufacturers responsible for the disposal of their products at end-of-life. Which concept from the course materials best supports this campaign?
option_a: The idea that consumers alone should bear disposal costs
option_b: E-waste management as an ethical and environmental responsibility required throughout technology lifecycles
option_c: The principle that hardware obsolescence should be accelerated
option_d: The notion that e-waste has no significant environmental impact
rationale: The course materials identify ethical and environmental responsibilities as core to the social science perspective on computing, supporting the principle that responsibility for e-waste extends across the entire technology lifecycle including manufacturing and post-consumer disposal.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A developing nation becomes a dumping ground for discarded electronics from wealthy countries. Local communities report soil and water contamination. Which analysis best describes this situation using course concepts?
option_a: This is a natural consequence of technological progress
option_b: This demonstrates how the Global Tech Era's rapid hardware obsolescence in developed nations creates disproportionate environmental burdens in developing regions, exacerbating the digital divide
option_c: This is beneficial because it provides free electronics to local residents
option_d: This situation has no connection to computing concepts
rationale: The situation connects multiple course concepts: hardware obsolescence from the Global Tech Era generates e-waste that disproportionately affects developing regions, while the digital divide means these regions lack the infrastructure to manage imported waste safely, compounding existing inequalities.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why simply shipping e-waste to recycling facilities abroad does not fully address the ethical concerns surrounding hardware disposal?
option_a: Recycling facilities abroad are always less effective
option_b: International shipping of waste is illegal everywhere
option_c: Even at recycling facilities, improper handling can expose workers to toxic substances, and the social science perspective highlights labour rights violations in the processing of discarded electronics
option_d: E-waste does not contain any valuable recyclable materials
rationale: The course materials note that social sciences examine labour rights violations in hardware manufacturing and processing. This critical perspective reveals that the ethical concerns extend beyond environmental contamination to include working conditions and labour protections in the recycling industry itself.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A consumer argues that buying the latest smartphone every year is a personal choice with no broader consequences. Which evaluation best assesses this position?
option_a: This is correct because individual consumption has no collective impact
option_b: This is justified because manufacturers design phones to last only one year
option_c: This is irrelevant to computing studies
option_d: This position ignores the collective impact of hardware obsolescence, which generates toxic e-waste that leaches into ecosystems and raises ethical questions about sustainable consumption in the Global Tech Era
rationale: The course materials connect individual technology consumption to broader environmental and ethical outcomes through the concepts of hardware obsolescence and toxic contamination. Personal choices aggregate into systemic environmental and social impacts that the social science perspective of computing explicitly examines.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which computing discipline bridges the gap between computing technology and business operations to optimise organisational efficiency?
option_a: Information Systems
option_b: Computer Science
option_c: Cyber Security
option_d: Data Science and Artificial Intelligence
rationale: Information Systems is explicitly defined as the discipline that bridges the gap between computing and business operations to optimise efficiency, distinguishing it from the theoretically focused or security-focused alternatives.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A company develops algorithms to detect fraudulent transactions in real-time by analysing patterns in millions of financial records. Which combination of computing disciplines is most directly involved in this project?
option_a: Only Cyber Security
option_b: Data Science and Artificial Intelligence for pattern analysis, and Cyber Security for protecting the transaction data
option_c: Only Information Systems
option_d: Only Software Engineering
rationale: Fraud detection requires both analytical capability (Data Science and AI for pattern recognition across large datasets) and protective measures (Cyber Security for securing financial transaction data). The course materials define both disciplines and their respective roles in such applications.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A national healthcare authority wants to implement a system that secures patient records, analyses health trends across populations, and integrates with existing hospital administrative software. Which three computing disciplines must collaborate for this project to succeed?
option_a: Only Computer Science, Software Engineering, and Information Systems
option_b: Only Cyber Security and Data Science
option_c: Cyber Security for data protection, Data Science and AI for health trend analysis, and Information Systems for administrative integration
option_d: Only Cyber Security, as patient data protection is the only requirement
rationale: The project has three distinct requirements that map directly to three computing disciplines: data protection (Cyber Security), population health analysis (Data Science and AI), and administrative system integration (Information Systems). The course materials define each discipline's scope, making this interdisciplinary collaboration essential.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A university wants to create a new undergraduate degree that teaches students how to design, build, and maintain large-scale software applications using systematic engineering principles. Which computing discipline should form the core curriculum?
option_a: Computer Science
option_b: Information Systems
option_c: Cyber Security
option_d: Software Engineering
rationale: Software Engineering is defined as involving the design, developing, and maintaining of software systems. Creating a degree focused on systematic principles for large-scale software development directly aligns with this discipline's core mandate.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why computing disciplines necessitate government policies on both workforce retraining and ethical governance?
option_a: Computing disciplines drive digital transformation that displaces traditional jobs while creating new ones requiring different skills, and their applications in sectors like healthcare and finance raise ethical questions about data use and automation
option_b: Government policies are unnecessary because the technology sector is self-regulating
option_c: Only workforce retraining is needed because ethics is not relevant to computing
option_d: Computing disciplines only affect the technology sector and have no broader societal impact
rationale: The course materials state that computing disciplines shape productivity in sectors like healthcare and finance while creating new job opportunities and displacing traditional roles, necessitating both workforce retraining policies and ethical governance frameworks.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best distinguishes Software Engineering from Data Science and Artificial Intelligence?
option_a: They are the same discipline with different names
option_b: Software Engineering focuses on designing, developing, and maintaining software systems, while Data Science and AI focus on analysing large datasets to extract insights and make predictions
option_c: Software Engineering does not involve any coding
option_d: Data Science and AI do not use software
rationale: The course materials define Software Engineering as concerned with the design, development, and maintenance of software systems, while Data Science and AI are defined as analysing large datasets for insights and predictions. These are fundamentally different activities within the computing field.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Some argue that Cyber Security is merely a subfield of Software Engineering rather than a distinct discipline. Which evaluation best addresses this claim?
option_a: This is correct because both involve software development
option_b: This is irrelevant because security is not important
option_c: This is incorrect because Cyber Security has a distinct focus on protecting data, systems, and networks from attacks, which requires specialised knowledge that extends beyond general software engineering principles
option_d: This is true because all software engineers are automatically security experts
rationale: While related, Cyber Security and Software Engineering have distinct core mandates. Cyber Security specifically focuses on protection of data, systems, and networks from cyber attacks — a specialisation that requires knowledge of threat landscapes, defensive strategies, and protective protocols beyond general software development.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A city government wants to digitise all its public services, including online tax filing, digital health records, and automated traffic management. Which statement best describes the disciplinary requirements of this initiative?
option_a: Only Software Engineering is needed to build the applications
option_b: Only Information Systems is needed because it is a government project
option_c: Only Cyber Security is needed because government data must be protected
option_d: Multiple computing disciplines are required, as the project spans software development, data security, systems integration, and data analytics
rationale: Comprehensive digital transformation initiatives require interdisciplinary collaboration across computing disciplines: Software Engineering for application development, Cyber Security for data protection, Information Systems for administrative integration, and Data Science for analytics on traffic patterns and service usage.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Why do the course materials describe computing disciplines as driving technological advancements, automation, and artificial intelligence rather than merely supporting them?
option_a: Because these disciplines are the foundational fields that create and advance the technologies rather than simply using existing tools
option_b: Because they only support technologies created by other fields
option_c: Because automation and AI are unrelated to computing
option_d: Because computing disciplines are only academic categories with no practical impact
rationale: Computing disciplines are described as driving advancements because they are the foundational creative fields — computer science develops algorithms, software engineering builds systems, and data science creates analytical models — that actively generate technological progress rather than passively supporting it.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nA tech entrepreneur claims that learning any single computing discipline is sufficient for a successful career in technology. Which evaluation best assesses this advice?
option_a: This is excellent advice because specialisation is always superior
option_b: This is misleading because real-world technology problems increasingly require interdisciplinary knowledge across multiple computing disciplines
option_c: This is correct because all computing disciplines are identical
option_d: This is irrelevant because careers in technology do not require formal disciplinary knowledge
rationale: The course materials demonstrate that computing disciplines influence diverse sectors and that complex technological challenges require collaboration across specialisations. Modern tech careers increasingly demand fluency in multiple disciplinary areas rather than isolated expertise.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which category of software manages hardware operations and provides a platform for running other software?
option_a: Application Software
option_b: Utility Software
option_c: System Software
option_d: Programming Software
rationale: System Software is explicitly defined as the category that manages hardware operations, with examples including MS DOS and Windows 10, distinguishing it from application and utility software categories.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A user installs an antivirus programme after noticing suspicious activity on her computer. Which category of software has she installed, and what is its primary purpose?
option_a: System Software, to replace the operating system
option_b: Application Software, to perform a specific user task
option_c: Programming Software, to write code
option_d: Utility Software, to provide maintenance and security functions for the computer system
rationale: Utility Software is defined as providing maintenance and security functions, with antivirus programmes given as the explicit example. This distinguishes utility software from system software (which manages hardware) and application software (which performs user-specific tasks).

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A student uses Microsoft Word to write an essay, MS DOS to manage file operations, and an antivirus tool to scan for malware. Which statement correctly classifies these three programmes?
option_a: Microsoft Word is Application Software, MS DOS is System Software, and the antivirus is Utility Software
option_b: All three are Application Software
option_c: All three are System Software
option_d: Microsoft Word is System Software, MS DOS is Utility Software, and the antivirus is Application Software
rationale: The course materials provide specific examples: Microsoft Word is listed as Application Software (performs specific tasks for the user), MS DOS and Windows 10 are System Software (manage hardware operations), and Antivirus is Utility Software (provides maintenance and security).

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A computer virus corrupts a user's important documents. Which statement best explains why the virus affected the documents but not the computer's physical components?
option_a: Viruses damage both software and hardware equally
option_b: Viruses only affect hardware
option_c: Documents are hardware, not software
option_d: Computer viruses corrupt software codes or instructions but do not affect hardware, because software is a set of coded instructions while hardware consists of physical components
rationale: The course materials explicitly state that computer viruses do not affect hardware but can corrupt software codes or instructions. This reflects the fundamental distinction between physical hardware components and intangible software instructions.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: An ATM freezes and displays an error message during a transaction. A technician determines that the ATM's hardware is functioning correctly. Which analysis most likely identifies the source of the problem?
option_a: The physical buttons are broken
option_b: The screen hardware has failed
option_c: The software controlling the ATM's operations has malfunctioned, since the hardware is confirmed to be working
option_d: The ATM needs more RAM installed
rationale: The course materials state that software controls the functionality of computing devices including ATMs, handling jobs according to specific programming. If hardware is functioning correctly, the malfunction must originate in the software layer that coordinates ATM operations.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why software is described as "a set of codes or programmes involving a combination of well defined instruction, scripts, procedures, documentation and data" rather than simply "instructions for a computer"?
option_a: The longer description is unnecessary
option_b: Software does not actually include instructions
option_c: Simple instructions alone cannot manipulate and execute pre-defined tasks; software requires scripts, procedures, documentation, and data working together as an integrated system
option_d: Documentation and data are not part of software
rationale: The comprehensive definition reflects that software is a complex system comprising multiple elements — instructions, scripts, procedures, documentation, and data — that collectively enable a computer system to perform predefined tasks, rather than being merely a list of simple commands.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A user claims that because application software performs specific tasks, it is more important than system software. Which evaluation best addresses this claim?
option_a: This is incorrect because system software is foundational — without it managing hardware operations and providing a platform, no application software could run at all
option_b: This is correct because users directly interact with application software
option_c: Both categories are the same and equally unimportant
option_d: System software is only needed for advanced users
rationale: System Software manages hardware operations and provides the platform on which all other software runs. Without this foundational layer, Application Software cannot function, establishing a hierarchical dependency that makes system software indispensable regardless of user visibility.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which phase of the software production process involves the actual writing of code by developers?
option_a: Requirement Gathering and Analysis
option_b: Implementation
option_c: Testing
option_d: Design Phase
rationale: Implementation is explicitly defined as the phase in which the actual coding of the software occurs, distinguishing it from the preceding design phase and the subsequent testing phase.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A project manager meets with stakeholders to document functional requirements for a new banking application. Which phase of software production does this activity represent?
option_a: Implementation
option_b: Testing
option_c: Requirement Gathering and Analysis
option_d: Design Phase
rationale: Requirement Gathering and Analysis is defined as the phase involving the defining of functional and non-functional requirements with stakeholders. Meeting with stakeholders to document requirements is the core activity of this initial phase.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A software team has completed coding a new feature. Before releasing it to users, they conduct unit testing, integration testing, and User Acceptance Testing (UAT). Which phase of software production encompasses these activities?
option_a: Requirement Gathering and Analysis
option_b: Design Phase
option_c: Implementation
option_d: Testing
rationale: Testing is defined as including unit testing, integration testing, and User Acceptance Testing (UAT). These activities occur after implementation to verify that the software functions correctly before deployment.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A software company releases version 1.0 of its product, then over the following year releases three update patches to fix bugs reported by users. Which characteristic of software production does this demonstrate?
option_a: Continuous maintenance is necessary to keep software functional over time, even after deployment
option_b: The initial release was poorly planned
option_c: Software production ends at deployment
option_d: Bug fixes are not part of formal software production
rationale: The course materials identify continuous maintenance as necessary to keep software functional over time, explicitly countering the misconception that the production cycle ends at deployment. Post-release bug fixes exemplify this ongoing maintenance requirement.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why User Acceptance Testing (UAT) is conducted after unit and integration testing rather than before them?
option_a: UAT is less important than other testing types
option_b: Unit and integration testing verify that individual components and their interactions work correctly, establishing technical reliability before UAT validates that the complete system meets stakeholder requirements
option_c: UAT does not involve stakeholders
option_d: Testing order has no logical basis
rationale: The testing phase proceeds from component-level verification (unit testing) through interaction verification (integration testing) to stakeholder validation (UAT). This sequence ensures technical correctness before assessing whether the system meets user needs.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: An architect creates detailed diagrams showing how the modules of a new payroll system will interact, what data will flow between them, and how the database will be structured. Which phase of software production is this?
option_a: Implementation
option_b: Testing
option_c: Design Phase
option_d: Requirement Gathering and Analysis
rationale: The Design Phase is defined as creating plans that lay out the structure of the system. Creating diagrams showing module interactions, data flows, and database structure is the defining activity of this phase.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A startup decides to skip the formal Design Phase and begin coding immediately based on informal discussions with potential users. Which evaluation best assesses this approach?
option_a: This is an excellent approach that saves time
option_b: This is standard practice in agile development
option_c: The Design Phase is optional for all projects
option_d: This is risky because the Design Phase creates the structural plan for the system; skipping it often leads to architectural problems, costly rework, and misalignment between the implemented system and stakeholder requirements
rationale: The Design Phase creates plans laying out the structure of the system. Skipping this phase eliminates the architectural blueprint that guides implementation, increasing the risk of structural defects and requirement misalignment that become increasingly costly to fix in later phases.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which software distribution model allows users to modify the source code freely and is available to the public at no cost?
option_a: Open Source Software
option_b: Proprietary Strategy
option_c: Subscription Based Software
option_d: Premium (Freemium) Software
rationale: Open Source Software is defined as freely available to the public with source code that can be modified, distinguishing it from proprietary, subscription, and freemium models that impose various restrictions on access or modification.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best contrasts proprietary software with open-source software?
option_a: Both allow unrestricted source code modification
option_b: Proprietary software is owned and licensed by companies requiring purchase with restricted source code, while open-source software allows public access and modification of its source code
option_c: Open-source software is always more expensive than proprietary software
option_d: Proprietary software cannot be purchased and is only available illegally
rationale: The course materials explicitly contrast open-source software (which allows source code modification) with proprietary software (which is strictly licensed and restricted), highlighting the fundamental difference in access rights and ownership.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A user downloads a word processor that offers basic formatting for free but requires monthly payment to access advanced features like mail merge and citation management. Which distribution model is this?
option_a: Open Source Software
option_b: Proprietary Strategy
option_c: Premium (Freemium) Software
option_d: Subscription Based Software
rationale: Premium (Freemium) Software is defined as offering basic features for free while users pay for premium features. The described model — free basic functionality with paid advanced features — matches this definition precisely.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A small business is choosing between Microsoft Office (proprietary, one-time purchase) and a cloud-based subscription office suite. Which evaluation best supports selecting the subscription model?
option_a: Proprietary software is always superior
option_b: Subscription models never provide value
option_c: The business should avoid both options
option_d: Subscription-based software provides continuous access to updates and new features, which may offer better long-term value for a business that needs to stay current with technology
rationale: Subscription Based Software charges users periodically for continuous access. For businesses requiring up-to-date features and security patches, the continuous update model may provide better value than a one-time purchase that could become outdated.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why the historic model of physical distribution (CDs, DVDs) has largely been replaced by digital distribution over the internet?
option_a: Digital distribution is faster and more cost-effective than manufacturing, packaging, and shipping physical media
option_b: Physical distribution was never used for software
option_c: Digital distribution is always more expensive
option_d: CDs and DVDs cannot store software
rationale: The course materials state that digital distribution has largely replaced physical distribution because it is faster and more cost-effective, reflecting the economic and logistical advantages of internet-based delivery over physical manufacturing and shipping.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A developer wants to release a programme that maximises accessibility for students in developing countries who have limited budgets but need to study the source code for educational purposes. Which distribution model best serves these needs?
option_a: Proprietary Strategy
option_b: Open Source Software
option_c: Subscription Based Software
option_d: Premium (Freemium) Software
rationale: Open Source Software is freely available and allows source code modification, making it both financially accessible and educationally valuable for students who need to examine how software is constructed. The other models impose costs or restrictions that would limit access.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: Which component of workforce planning involves preparing the internal pipeline for future leadership roles within an IT organisation?
option_a: Talent Acquisition
option_b: Employee Retention
option_c: Succession Planning
option_d: Skills Gap Analysis
rationale: Succession Planning is explicitly defined as preparing the internal pipeline for future leadership roles, distinguishing it from talent acquisition (hiring) and employee retention (keeping current staff).

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: An IT company notices that many of its senior developers are approaching retirement age. The company begins mentoring mid-level developers for future team lead positions. Which workforce planning component does this represent?
option_a: Talent Acquisition
option_b: Employee Retention
option_c: Digital Skills Gap remediation
option_d: Succession Planning
rationale: Succession Planning involves preparing the internal pipeline for future leadership roles. Mentoring mid-level developers to replace retiring senior staff is a direct implementation of succession planning.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A tech startup specialising in cloud computing is struggling to find qualified engineers with cloud architecture expertise. Which workforce planning component is most directly relevant to addressing this challenge?
option_a: Talent Acquisition, because it involves hiring specialists for emerging areas like AI and cloud computing
option_b: Succession Planning, because the current engineers will retire soon
option_c: Employee Retention, because the existing engineers want to leave
option_d: Digital Divide reduction
rationale: Talent Acquisition is defined as hiring specialists for emerging areas like AI and cloud computing. The challenge of finding qualified cloud architecture engineers directly falls under this component of workforce planning.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A multinational technology firm offers competitive salaries, remote work options, and professional development budgets to prevent its key engineers from joining competitors. Which workforce planning component does this strategy represent?
option_a: Talent Acquisition
option_b: Employee Retention
option_c: Succession Planning
option_d: Workforce displacement
rationale: Employee Retention is defined as keeping qualified staff in a competitive environment. Offering competitive benefits to prevent employees from leaving directly implements this retention-focused component of workforce planning.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: Which analysis best explains why workforce planning in IT is distinct from general human resource management?
option_a: IT workforce planning is identical to general HR
option_b: General HR is more complex than IT workforce planning
option_c: IT workforce planning specifically focuses on rapid technological evolution and the specialised skill gaps that emerge as new technologies displace existing competencies
option_d: IT professionals do not require workforce planning
rationale: The course materials indicate that IT workforce planning is distinct from general HR in its specific focus on rapid technological evolution and IT skill gaps. The pace of technological change in computing creates unique workforce challenges that general HR frameworks may not address adequately.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: Which analysis best explains why all three components of IT workforce planning — talent acquisition, succession planning, and employee retention — are necessary simultaneously rather than sequentially?
option_a: Organisations only need to focus on one component at a time
option_b: Workforce planning is unnecessary in the technology sector
option_c: The components are interchangeable and can replace one another
option_d: A competitive IT environment requires continuously bringing in new specialists, preparing future leaders, and keeping current expertise — any gap in one area creates vulnerability in the organisation's capability
rationale: The course materials define all three components as essential for ensuring organisations remain competitive in a fast-changing industry. Each addresses a different time horizon and risk: acquisition fills immediate gaps, succession ensures future leadership, and retention protects existing capability.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A government technology agency forecasts that within five years, half of its current IT staff will lack the skills needed for emerging AI-driven service delivery. Which workforce planning response best addresses this forecast?
option_a: A combination of talent acquisition for immediate AI expertise, succession planning for future leadership, and employee retention strategies for staff willing to reskill
option_b: Doing nothing because skills gaps resolve themselves
option_c: Firing all current staff and hiring new AI specialists
option_d: Relying solely on succession planning without considering current staff
rationale: A forecasted skills gap requires a multi-pronged approach: talent acquisition brings immediate capability, succession planning develops future leaders, and retention strategies encourage existing staff to adapt. No single component adequately addresses a workforce transition of this magnitude.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A company decides to address its IT skills gap exclusively by hiring contractors for all technical roles, eliminating permanent positions. Which course concept best explains the risk in this strategy?
option_a: This is ideal because contractors are always cheaper
option_b: This has no drawbacks
option_c: This is risky because it eliminates succession planning and employee retention capabilities, potentially leaving the organisation without institutional knowledge or leadership continuity
option_d: Permanent positions are never valuable in IT
rationale: Relying exclusively on contractors eliminates the internal pipeline development that succession planning provides and the institutional knowledge accumulation that retention fosters. While contractors may fill immediate gaps, the long-term organisational capability depends on developing and retaining internal expertise.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A university's IT department implements a mentorship programme pairing senior staff with new graduates, creates a leadership development track for high-performing analysts, and attends recruitment fairs to attract diverse candidates. Which workforce planning components are represented?
option_a: Only Employee Retention
option_b: Only Talent Acquisition
option_c: Talent Acquisition (recruitment fairs), Succession Planning (leadership track), and a foundation for Employee Retention (mentorship improving job satisfaction)
option_d: Only Succession Planning
rationale: The initiative spans all three workforce planning components: recruitment fairs address talent acquisition, the leadership development track implements succession planning, and mentorship programmes contribute to retention by enhancing job satisfaction and professional growth opportunities.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: Which analysis best explains why employee retention is particularly challenging in the IT industry compared to other sectors?
option_a: IT professionals are less skilled than workers in other sectors
option_b: The IT industry does not need to retain employees
option_c: IT professionals prefer unemployment
option_d: The fast-changing nature of technology creates constant demand for new skills, making qualified IT professionals highly sought after by competitors and increasing turnover pressure
rationale: The course materials describe the IT environment as fast-changing and competitive, with high demand for skilled professionals. This dynamic creates a labour market where qualified staff have many opportunities, making retention challenging without proactive strategies.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: What is the primary purpose of workforce planning in IT?
option_a: Forecasting labour demands, identifying skill gaps, and implementing strategies to ensure a steady supply of qualified professionals
option_b: Reducing the number of IT professionals in the workforce
option_c: Eliminating the need for training programmes
option_d: Replacing all human workers with automation
rationale: Workforce Planning in IT is explicitly defined as forecasting labour demands, identifying skill gaps, and implementing strategies to ensure a steady supply of qualified professionals — a proactive approach to managing human capital in a rapidly evolving field.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A technology firm identifies that its competitors are offering significantly higher salaries for cloud security specialists. Which workforce planning component should the firm prioritise in response?
option_a: Succession Planning, because all current staff will retire
option_b: Employee Retention, because the competitive environment threatens to lure away qualified staff, and retention strategies are needed to keep them
option_c: Talent Acquisition is the only response needed
option_d: The firm should ignore competitor salary offers
rationale: Employee Retention is defined as keeping qualified staff in a competitive environment. When competitors offer higher compensation for specialised roles, retention strategies become critical to prevent loss of existing expertise.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: Which analysis best explains the relationship between emerging technologies and workforce planning in IT?
option_a: Emerging technologies have no impact on workforce needs
option_b: Workforce planning becomes unnecessary as technology advances
option_c: Emerging technologies like AI and cloud computing create new specialisations that did not previously exist, requiring workforce planning to forecast demands and develop or acquire those new competencies
option_d: Emerging technologies eliminate the need for human IT professionals
rationale: The course materials link workforce planning to emerging areas like AI and cloud computing, indicating that technological advancement continuously creates new specialisations that workforce planning must anticipate and address through acquisition, development, or retention strategies.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A company claims that because the computing field evolves so rapidly, long-term workforce planning is futile and organisations should simply react to needs as they arise. Which evaluation best assesses this position?
option_a: This is correct because the future is entirely unpredictable
option_b: This is the most efficient approach to IT staffing
option_c: This is supported by the course materials
option_d: This is short-sighted because proactive workforce planning is specifically designed to anticipate changes in a fast-changing industry; reactive approaches lead to skill shortages, delayed projects, and loss of competitive advantage
rationale: The course materials define workforce planning as forecasting labour demands and implementing strategies to ensure a steady supply of qualified professionals. This proactive approach is particularly important in a fast-changing industry where reactive hiring cannot keep pace with evolving skill requirements.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A hospital's IT department needs to ensure it can maintain its electronic health record system even if key personnel leave. Which combination of workforce planning components best addresses this need?
