option_a: Succession Planning to develop backup expertise, Employee Retention to keep current specialists, and cross-training to distribute knowledge
option_b: Talent Acquisition to replace staff after they leave
option_c: Doing nothing because health record systems are self-maintaining
option_d: Outsourcing all IT functions immediately
rationale: Ensuring continuity of critical systems requires Succession Planning to develop internal backup expertise, Employee Retention to maintain current knowledge holders, and cross-training to prevent single points of failure. Talent acquisition alone is insufficient because new hires require time to develop system-specific knowledge.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A fintech company wants to expand into blockchain technology but discovers that none of its current developers have relevant expertise. Which workforce planning actions should the company take first?
option_a: Fire all current developers and hire blockchain specialists
option_b: Talent Acquisition to hire blockchain specialists for immediate capability, combined with Succession Planning to develop internal blockchain expertise over time
option_c: Abandon the blockchain expansion plan
option_d: Wait for current developers to learn blockchain organically
rationale: The course materials define talent acquisition as hiring specialists for emerging areas. When entering a new technology domain, organisations need immediate expertise through acquisition while building long-term internal capability through succession-oriented development programmes.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A mid-sized company's IT department has high turnover among junior developers who report leaving for better career development opportunities elsewhere. Which workforce planning component should the company strengthen?
option_a: Talent Acquisition to continuously replace leaving staff
option_b: Reduce salaries to save money
option_c: Succession Planning, because it prepares the internal pipeline for future leadership roles, demonstrating clear career pathways that can improve retention
option_d: Eliminate junior developer positions
rationale: When staff leave due to limited career development, Succession Planning addresses the root cause by creating visible leadership pathways and development opportunities. This proactive approach both develops future leaders and improves retention by demonstrating long-term career potential.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: An organisation measures its IT workforce planning success solely by the number of positions filled each quarter. Which evaluation best assesses this metric?
option_a: This is the only metric that matters
option_b: This is comprehensive because filling positions is the sole purpose of workforce planning
option_c: This approach captures the quality of hires and long-term capability development
option_d: This is inadequate because it ignores critical dimensions like retention rates, leadership pipeline strength, skill gap closure, and the alignment of workforce capabilities with strategic technology goals
rationale: Workforce planning encompasses forecasting labour demands, identifying skill gaps, and implementing strategies across talent acquisition, succession planning, and retention. Measuring only position fills ignores the quality, retention, and strategic alignment of the workforce.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: Which analysis best explains how workforce planning in IT contributes to organisational competitiveness?
option_a: By ensuring a steady supply of qualified professionals with emerging skills, workforce planning prevents skill shortages that could delay projects, compromise security, or prevent adoption of competitive technologies
option_b: Workforce planning has no impact on competitiveness
option_c: Workforce planning reduces competitiveness by increasing labour costs
option_d: Only talent acquisition affects competitiveness
rationale: The course materials state that workforce planning ensures organisations remain competitive in a fast-changing industry by managing highly-skilled IT talent. Skill shortages directly impact project delivery, security posture, and technology adoption — all critical to competitive positioning.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Human Resources in Computing and IT Professions
stem: A national government launches a programme to train 10,000 citizens in cloud computing, AI, and cybersecurity over three years. Which workforce planning challenge is this programme most directly addressing?
option_a: Employee Retention in existing companies
option_b: Forecasted labour demands and skill gaps in emerging technology areas, ensuring a steady supply of qualified IT professionals for the national economy
option_c: Succession Planning for specific government departments
option_d: Hardware obsolescence
rationale: Large-scale training initiatives in emerging technology areas address the foundational workforce planning challenge of ensuring a steady supply of qualified professionals. By developing domestic capability in high-demand fields, the programme responds to forecasted labour demands and identified skill gaps.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which sector has been revolutionised by electronic health records and diagnostic tools as part of information processing transformation?
option_a: Health Care
option_b: Agriculture
option_c: Construction
option_d: Transportation
rationale: Health Care is explicitly identified as one of the sectors revolutionised by information processing, specifically through electronic health records and diagnostic tools that have transformed medical practice.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: What is one key way information processing has transformed economic transactions?
option_a: By requiring all transactions to occur in physical banks
option_b: By streamlining economic transactions without requiring physical presence
option_c: By eliminating all forms of digital payment
option_d: By making transactions slower but more secure
rationale: The course materials state that information processing has streamlined economic transactions without physical presence, enabling individuals to conduct transactions without banking in person.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: During an election, political parties use social media platforms to spread campaign messages and mobilise voters. Which component of information roles in society does this demonstrate?
option_a: Economic Growth
option_b: Health Care
option_c: Politics
option_d: Communication and Connectivity
rationale: Politics is identified as one of the key domains where information processing has had revolutionary impact, specifically through the spread of information during electioneering and voting processes.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A patient in a rural clinic receives a diagnosis within hours because her test results are analysed by a specialist in a distant city via digital systems. Which information role enables this outcome?
option_a: Economic transaction processing
option_b: Political information dissemination
option_c: Social media connectivity
option_d: Health Care information processing through electronic records and diagnostic tools
rationale: Health Care is identified as a sector revolutionised by information processing through electronic health records and diagnostic tools. The scenario demonstrates how digital diagnostic capabilities accelerate medical diagnosis regardless of geographic distance.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which statement best explains why the course materials describe information processing as having "revolutionised the global space"?
option_a: It has created instant connections, transformed data management, and birthed new technologies across multiple societal domains simultaneously
option_b: It has only affected developed countries
option_c: It has made information less accessible than before
option_d: It has eliminated the need for human communication
rationale: The course materials define information roles as revolutionising the global space through instant connections, data management, and the birthing of new technologies — a comprehensive transformation spanning communication, health, politics, and economic domains.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A small business owner begins selling her products through an e-commerce website and processes payments through mobile money platforms. Which information role best describes this transformation?
option_a: Health Care modernisation
option_b: Economic Growth through streamlined transactions without physical presence
option_c: Political engagement
option_d: Communication and Connectivity
rationale: The course materials identify Economic Growth as a domain where information processing streamlines economic transactions without physical presence. The business owner's shift to e-commerce and mobile payments exemplifies this transformation.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A doctor uses telemedicine to consult with patients in remote areas, accessing their electronic health records in real-time during video calls. Which combination of information roles does this scenario demonstrate?
option_a: Only Communication and Connectivity
option_b: Only Economic Growth
option_c: Health Care (electronic records and diagnostics) and Communication and Connectivity (instant global connections via the internet)
option_d: Only Politics
rationale: Telemedicine combines Health Care information processing (electronic health records and diagnostic tools) with Communication and Connectivity (instant global connections via the internet). Both domains are identified in the course materials as revolutionised by information processing.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nA government implements an online voting system that allows citizens to cast ballots from any location with internet access. Which information roles are primarily demonstrated?
option_a: Only Health Care
option_b: Only Economic Growth
option_c: Communication and Connectivity and Politics
option_d: Both Communication and Connectivity (internet access) and Politics (information spread during voting)
rationale: Online voting demonstrates Politics through the application of information technology to the electoral process, and Communication and Connectivity through the internet infrastructure that enables remote participation. Both are explicitly identified as information roles in society.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nA university offers fully online degree programmes to students across multiple continents, with lectures delivered via video conferencing and assessments submitted through digital platforms. Which information role best explains the viability of this educational model?
option_a: Communication and Connectivity through instant global connections via the internet
option_b: Health Care through electronic records
option_c: Politics through information spread
option_d: Economic Growth through banking
rationale: The global delivery of online education relies on Communication and Connectivity — instant global connections via the internet — which the course materials identify as a revolutionary information role. Without this connectivity infrastructure, cross-continental online education would not be feasible.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nA disaster response agency uses social media to coordinate relief efforts, share real-time updates with affected communities, and process donations through online platforms. Which information roles are demonstrated?
option_a: Only Health Care
option_b: Communication and Connectivity (coordination and updates) and Economic Growth (online donation processing)
option_c: Only Politics
option_d: Only Communication and Connectivity
rationale: The scenario demonstrates Communication and Connectivity through instant coordination and information sharing, and Economic Growth through streamlined online donation transactions. Both are identified in the course materials as domains transformed by information processing.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nA researcher notes that countries with robust digital infrastructure experienced smaller economic disruptions during the pandemic because businesses could transition to online operations. Which information role best explains this resilience?
option_a: Health Care adaptation
option_b: Political stability
option_c: Economic Growth through streamlined transactions without physical presence
option_d: Communication for entertainment
rationale: The ability to conduct transactions without physical presence — a defining characteristic of Economic Growth through information processing — enabled business continuity during pandemic restrictions. Countries with robust digital infrastructure could leverage this information role to maintain economic activity.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nWhich analysis best explains why information processing in Health Care accelerates medical diagnosis processes?
option_a: It replaces doctors with computers
option_b: It eliminates the need for medical training
option_c: It slows down diagnosis but improves accuracy
option_d: Electronic health records enable instant access to patient history, and diagnostic tools allow rapid analysis of test data, reducing the time needed to reach diagnostic conclusions
rationale: The course materials state that information processing in Health Care accelerates medical diagnosis processes. Electronic records eliminate delays in accessing patient history, while digital diagnostic tools process test data faster than manual methods.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nWhich analysis best distinguishes how information processing has transformed politics compared to its transformation of economic transactions?
option_a: Politics has been transformed by the spread of information during electioneering and voting, while economic transactions have been transformed by eliminating the need for physical presence in banking and commerce
option_b: Both have been transformed in identical ways
option_c: Only politics has been affected; economic transactions remain unchanged
option_d: Information processing has had no impact on either domain
rationale: The course materials identify distinct transformations: Politics through information spread during electioneering and voting, and Economic Growth through streamlining transactions without physical presence. Each domain has been revolutionised through different mechanisms of information processing.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nWhich analysis best explains the relationship between Communication and Connectivity and the other information roles identified in the course materials?
option_a: Communication and Connectivity is independent of other information roles
option_b: Communication and Connectivity serves as the foundational infrastructure that enables the other roles — Health Care, Politics, and Economic Growth — to function through instant global connections
option_c: Communication and Connectivity has replaced the other information roles
option_d: The other information roles do not require connectivity
rationale: Communication and Connectivity (instant global connections via the internet) provides the underlying infrastructure for all other information roles. Electronic health records, political information dissemination, and online economic transactions all depend on this connectivity foundation.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nWhich analysis best explains why information processing is described as having "birthed new technologies" rather than merely improving existing ones?
option_a: Information processing only improves old technologies
option_b: New technologies are unrelated to information processing
option_c: The transformation of data management and global connectivity created entirely new technological categories — such as telemedicine, e-commerce platforms, and digital political campaigns — that did not exist in prior technological eras
option_d: Information processing has had no technological impact
rationale: The course materials describe information processing as birthing new technologies because the revolution in data management and connectivity created novel technological categories and applications that were not possible before the information age.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nA developing nation has limited internet infrastructure. Which analysis best predicts the likely consequences for its health care, political, and economic sectors based on the course framework?
option_a: These sectors will function better without information technology
option_b: Limited connectivity has no impact on societal development
option_c: Only the health care sector will be affected
option_d: Without robust Communication and Connectivity infrastructure, the nation cannot fully realise the transformative benefits of information processing in Health Care, Politics, and Economic Growth
rationale: The course materials demonstrate that Communication and Connectivity is the enabling infrastructure for all other information roles. Limited connectivity constrains the ability to implement electronic health records, digital political processes, and online economic transactions.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nWhich analysis best explains how the four identified information roles are interdependent rather than operating independently?
option_a: Health Care relies on Connectivity for telemedicine, Economic Growth uses Health Care data for insurance and research, Politics shapes regulation for all digital domains, and Connectivity enables all of them
option_b: The roles operate entirely independently
option_c: Only Health Care and Economic Growth are related
option_d: The roles are mutually exclusive
rationale: The four information roles form an interconnected ecosystem: Connectivity enables Health Care telemedicine; Health Care data feeds into Economic Growth through medical billing and research; Politics creates the regulatory framework; and all depend on the connectivity infrastructure.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nA policy maker argues that investing in digital infrastructure is less important than investing in traditional transportation infrastructure because both connect people. Which evaluation best assesses this argument?
option_a: This is correct because transportation and digital infrastructure are identical
option_b: This overlooks that digital infrastructure uniquely enables instantaneous global connection, remote health care, online economic transactions, and digital political participation that physical transportation cannot provide
option_c: Digital infrastructure has no advantages over transportation
option_d: Both should never be invested in simultaneously
rationale: While transportation connects people physically, Communication and Connectivity through digital infrastructure enables instant global connections that transcend geographic barriers. The course materials highlight capabilities — online transactions, remote diagnosis, digital political engagement — that transportation infrastructure cannot replicate.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nA country bans all social media platforms citing political concerns. Which evaluation best assesses the likely impact on the information roles identified in the course materials?
option_a: This will have no impact on any information role
option_b: This only affects political information dissemination
option_c: This restricts Communication and Connectivity infrastructure, which will negatively affect Health Care (telemedicine coordination), Economic Growth (online commerce), and Politics (legitimate information spread) beyond the stated political concerns
option_d: This improves all information roles by reducing misinformation
rationale: Since Communication and Connectivity is the foundational infrastructure for all information roles, restricting social media platforms disrupts not only political communication but also health care coordination, economic transactions, and general connectivity that the course materials identify as transformative societal roles.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: nAn economist claims that the digital transformation of economic transactions has eliminated all barriers to financial inclusion. Which evaluation best assesses this claim?
option_a: This is correct because everyone now has equal digital access
option_b: This is accurate because the digital divide no longer exists
option_c: This is supported by all available evidence
option_d: This is overstated because the digital divide means many people still lack access to the connectivity infrastructure required for online economic transactions, so barriers to financial inclusion persist
rationale: The digital divide — the gap between those with access to modern computing technologies and those without — directly contradicts the claim that digital transformation has eliminated all barriers. Without connectivity access, online economic transactions remain unavailable to disadvantaged populations.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which type of business communication flows from a subordinate to a manager up the organisational hierarchy?
option_a: Upward Communication
option_b: Downward Communication
option_c: Lateral Communication
option_d: External Communication
rationale: Upward Communication is explicitly defined as communication from a subordinate to a manager up the organisational hierarchy, distinguishing it from other communication flows.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A department head sends an email to her team announcing new performance targets and procedural changes. Which type of business communication is this?
option_a: Upward Communication
option_b: Downward Communication
option_c: Lateral Communication
option_d: External Communication
rationale: Downward Communication is defined as communication from a superior to a subordinate (managerial communication). A department head communicating targets and procedures to her team exemplifies this downward flow in the organisational hierarchy.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Two marketing managers from different product lines meet to coordinate a joint promotional campaign. Which type of business communication does this represent?
option_a: Upward Communication
option_b: Downward Communication
option_c: Lateral / Technical Communication
option_d: External Communication
rationale: Lateral / Technical Communication is defined as communication between co-workers or departments. Two managers at the same organisational level coordinating across product lines exemplifies this lateral flow.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A multinational corporation implements video conferencing systems and web-based collaboration platforms to connect its offices across three continents. Which business communication application does this demonstrate?
option_a: Only Upward Communication
option_b: Only Downward Communication
option_c: Only Lateral Communication
option_d: Web-based communication and video conferencing to ensure smooth commercial operations across geographic boundaries
rationale: The course materials identify web-based communication, telephone meetings, and video conferencing as applications of business communication technology that ensure smooth commercial operations. Multi-office connectivity exemplifies this application.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: An employee submits a quarterly report to his director summarising team achievements and requesting additional resources. Which type of business communication is this?
option_a: Upward Communication, because it flows from a subordinate to a manager up the organisational hierarchy
option_b: Downward Communication
option_c: Lateral Communication
option_d: External Communication
rationale: Upward Communication flows from subordinate to manager up the hierarchy. An employee submitting a report and request to a director represents this upward flow of information and requests.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A retail chain uses telephone meetings between regional managers and headquarters to discuss sales strategies. Which combination of communication types and applications is demonstrated?
option_a: Only Lateral Communication using web-based tools
option_b: Upward Communication (regional reports to headquarters) and Downward Communication (strategy directives to regions) using telephone meetings for commercial benefit
option_c: Only External Communication
option_d: Only informal communication
rationale: Regional managers communicating with headquarters involves both upward flow (regional performance data) and downward flow (strategic directives). Telephone meetings are identified as a business communication application for commercial operations.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best contrasts hierarchical communication flows (upward and downward) with peer-to-peer flows (lateral)?
option_a: They are identical in purpose and direction
option_b: Hierarchical flows are always less effective than lateral flows
option_c: Hierarchical flows follow the organisational chain of command for reporting and directives, while lateral flows facilitate coordination between peers and departments at the same organisational level
option_d: Lateral communication does not occur in organisations
rationale: The course materials explicitly contrast hierarchical communication flows (upward/downward) with peer-to-peer flows (lateral). Hierarchical flows follow the organisational structure, while lateral flows enable coordination across functional boundaries at equivalent levels.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why modern business communication relies on multiple channels — web-based, telephone, and video conferencing — rather than a single method?
option_a: Multiple channels create confusion and should be avoided
option_b: Only video conferencing is effective in modern business
option_c: A single channel is sufficient for all business needs
option_d: Different communication needs require different channels; urgent matters may need telephone calls, complex discussions need video conferencing, and routine updates use web-based platforms
rationale: The course materials list web-based communication, telephone meetings, and video conferencing as complementary methods for ensuring smooth commercial operations. Different contexts require different communication modalities.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains how computing technology has transformed business communication from traditional face-to-face methods?
option_a: Computing technology enables instant communication across geographic boundaries through web-based platforms, telephone meetings, and video conferencing, allowing organisations to operate smoothly without requiring physical co-location
option_b: Computing technology has eliminated the need for all communication
option_c: Face-to-face communication remains the only effective method
option_d: Computing technology has made business communication slower
rationale: The course materials identify web-based communication, telephone meetings, and video conferencing as computing-enabled methods that ensure smooth commercial operations, transcending the geographic limitations of face-to-face communication.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A manager insists that all team communication must occur through formal downward channels, prohibiting lateral communication between team members. Which evaluation best assesses this policy?
option_a: This is ideal because it ensures complete managerial control
option_b: This is counterproductive because lateral communication between co-workers and departments is essential for coordination, and prohibiting it creates bottlenecks and delays
option_c: Lateral communication has no value in organisations
option_d: This approach improves team efficiency
rationale: Lateral / Technical Communication between co-workers or departments is identified as a necessary type of business communication. Prohibiting it eliminates the coordination channels that enable efficient cross-functional collaboration.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: What is the primary purpose of Customer Relationship Management (CRM) systems in a business context?
option_a: To manufacture products more efficiently
option_b: To manage physical inventory levels
option_c: To manage and analyse customer interactions and data throughout the customer lifecycle
option_d: To replace all human employees with automation
rationale: CRM is explicitly defined as the combination of practices, strategies, and technologies that companies use to manage and analyse customer interactions and data throughout the customer lifecycle.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A retail company collects customer purchase history from its website, email responses, and social media interactions. Which CRM component does this activity represent?
option_a: Product development
option_b: Inventory management
option_c: Financial auditing
option_d: Data Compilation across channels like company websites, direct mail, and social media
rationale: Data Compilation is identified as the CRM component involving the gathering of customer data across channels such as company websites, direct mail, and social media. The scenario describes exactly this cross-channel data gathering.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A telecommunications company uses its CRM system to identify customers whose contracts expire within three months and sends them targeted retention offers. Which CRM objective is this achieving?
option_a: Customer retention by using relationship data to prevent churn
option_b: Inventory management
option_c: Employee recruitment
option_d: Product manufacturing
rationale: The course materials state that CRM is used to improve customer service relationships and assist in customer retention. Identifying at-risk customers and proactively offering retention incentives directly implements this objective.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A sales team uses CRM analytics to identify which customer segments generate the highest revenue and which products are most frequently purchased together. Which CRM application does this represent?
option_a: Inventory supervision
option_b: Driving sales and growth through customer data analysis
option_c: Employee performance management
option_d: Physical goods tracking
rationale: The course materials identify driving sales and growth as a CRM objective. Using analytics to identify high-value segments and cross-selling opportunities directly applies CRM data to revenue generation.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best distinguishes Customer Relationship Management from Inventory Management?
option_a: They are the same system with different names
option_b: CRM supervises physical goods while Inventory Management supervises customer data
option_c: CRM supervises relational data and customer interactions throughout the lifecycle, while Inventory Management supervises the physical flow of goods
option_d: Neither system uses data
rationale: The course materials explicitly contrast CRM (which supervises relational data and interactions) with Inventory Management (which supervises the physical flow of goods). These are fundamentally different business functions.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A small business owner claims that CRM systems are only useful for large corporations and offer no benefit to small enterprises. Which evaluation best assesses this claim?
option_a: This is correct because CRM requires massive infrastructure
option_b: Small businesses do not have customers to manage
option_c: CRM is only for multinational corporations
option_d: This is incorrect because CRM practices help any business improve customer relationships, assist in retention, and drive sales growth regardless of size
rationale: The course materials describe CRM as improving customer service relationships, assisting in customer retention, and driving sales growth — benefits that apply to businesses of all sizes. Small businesses can leverage CRM to compete effectively through superior customer understanding.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: An online bookstore tracks which books a customer browses, purchases, and reviews, then uses this data to recommend similar titles. Which CRM data source and application does this demonstrate?
option_a: Data compilation from website interactions used to improve customer relationships through personalised recommendations
option_b: Only inventory tracking
option_c: Only financial auditing
option_d: Only shipping logistics
rationale: Tracking browsing, purchase, and review behaviour represents data compilation from customer interactions. Using this data for personalised recommendations exemplifies CRM's purpose of managing and analysing customer data to improve relationships and drive sales.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why CRM data compilation must occur across multiple channels rather than relying on a single data source?
option_a: Single-channel data provides a complete customer view
option_b: Customers interact with businesses through websites, email, social media, and direct contact; compiling data across all channels creates a comprehensive understanding of the customer lifecycle
option_c: Multiple channels create data redundancy with no benefit
option_d: CRM only works with social media data
rationale: The course materials identify data compilation across channels (websites, direct mail, social media) as essential to CRM. Single-channel data captures only a fragment of customer behaviour, while multi-channel compilation enables a holistic view of the customer lifecycle.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A company implements a CRM system but fails to train its staff on how to use the customer data to improve service interactions. Which evaluation best assesses this situation?
option_a: The CRM system will automatically improve customer service without staff involvement
option_b: Technology alone guarantees relationship improvements
option_c: The company has collected valuable data but failed to complete the CRM objective of using that data to improve customer service relationships and drive growth
option_d: CRM data has no connection to customer service quality
rationale: CRM is defined as practices, strategies, and technologies used to manage and analyse customer interaction and data. Technology implementation without staff capability to leverage the data for service improvement represents an incomplete CRM deployment.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A business argues that because it has been successful without a formal CRM system for decades, it does not need one now. Which evaluation best assesses this position?
option_a: This is correct because traditional business methods are always superior
option_b: CRM systems are unnecessary for established businesses
option_c: Success in the past guarantees future success
option_d: This overlooks that modern customers expect personalised interactions across multiple channels, and competitors using CRM may gain advantages in customer retention and sales growth
rationale: The course materials describe CRM as managing and analysing customer interactions throughout the lifecycle to improve relationships and drive growth. In competitive markets, businesses without CRM capabilities may lose customers to competitors who can deliver more personalised, data-driven experiences.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which field examines how people interact with computers and how to design systems that are efficient, effective, and satisfying to use?
option_a: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)
option_b: Cyber Security
option_c: Software Engineering
option_d: Data Science
rationale: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is explicitly defined as the field that examines how people interact with computers and how to design systems that are efficient, effective, and satisfying to use.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A software team is designing a new mobile banking app. They conduct user interviews to understand how elderly customers currently manage their finances and what challenges they face with existing digital tools. Which HCI component does this research address?
option_a: Only technical functionality testing
option_b: Accessibility Needs and the social context of use, examining how demographics like age influence engagement with digital systems
option_c: Only aesthetic design preferences
option_d: Only security protocol development
rationale: The course materials identify Accessibility Needs as an HCI component examining how demographics (age, physical ability) influence engagement. Researching elderly users' challenges directly addresses this accessibility dimension of HCI.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A design team notices that users in different cultures interpret the colour red differently — as a warning in some contexts and as a celebratory colour in others. Which HCI component should guide their colour choices for a global application?
option_a: Only Cognitive Load
option_b: Only Accessibility Needs
option_c: The Social Context of Use, because sociology reveals that environments including culture affect user expectations and interpretations
option_d: Only technical performance metrics
rationale: The Social Context of Use is identified as the HCI component examining how environments (workplace, culture) affect expectations. Cultural colour symbolism differences directly illustrate how social context must inform interface design decisions.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best contrasts the pure technical functionalities of a system with the human factors that inform interface design?
option_a: Technical functionalities and human factors are identical
option_b: Human factors are irrelevant to system design
option_c: Technical functionalities determine all design decisions without human input
option_d: Pure technical functionalities address what the system can do, while human factors — including cognitive load, social context, and accessibility needs — determine how effectively and satisfyingly users can actually interact with those functionalities
rationale: The course materials contrast pure technical functionalities with human factors that inform interface design. HCI bridges this gap by examining how cognitive, social, and accessibility dimensions shape the user experience beyond mere technical capability.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: What does usability primarily assess about a user's interaction with a system?
option_a: How well a user can interact with a system to achieve specific goals effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily
option_b: Only the visual attractiveness of the interface
option_c: Only the technical speed of the system
option_d: Only the cost of developing the system
rationale: Usability is explicitly defined as referring to how well a user can interact with a system to achieve specific goals effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily — a comprehensive measure beyond aesthetics or speed alone.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A design team asks users to complete a series of tasks on a prototype website while observers record where users struggle and how long each task takes. Which usability evaluation method is this?
option_a: Heuristic Evaluation
option_b: Task-Based Testing
option_c: Interface Comparison
option_d: Automated Testing
rationale: Task-Based Testing is defined as observing users completing specific actions. The scenario describes exactly this method — observing users perform tasks to identify usability issues and measure task completion efficiency.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why usability testing must include participants from diverse demographic groups rather than only technical experts?
option_a: Technical experts are the only users who matter
option_b: Diverse participants make testing slower with no benefit
option_c: Demographics like age, physical ability, and cultural background influence how users interact with systems, and inclusive testing identifies barriers that homogeneous testing groups would miss
option_d: Usability is identical for all user groups
rationale: The course materials connect usability to HCI's examination of how demographics influence engagement. Testing with diverse participants ensures the system accommodates varied user needs, including those of marginalised or underrepresented groups.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why the digital skills gap is distinct from the digital divide?
option_a: They are identical concepts with different names
option_b: The digital skills gap refers to hardware quality while the digital divide refers to software quality
option_c: The digital divide focuses on the competence to use tools, while the skills gap focuses on physical access
option_d: The digital divide focuses on physical access to hardware and internet, while the digital skills gap focuses on the competence to use those tools effectively
rationale: The course materials explicitly contrast the two concepts: the digital divide focuses on physical access to hardware/internet, whereas the skills gap focuses on the competence to use those tools. This distinction is frequently confused but represents fundamentally different barriers.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A government provides free laptops to all citizens but offers no training on how to use them. Which course concept best applies to assessing this programme's likely effectiveness in addressing digital inequality?
option_a: This will completely eliminate digital inequality
option_b: Hardware access alone guarantees digital competence
option_c: This is insufficient because providing access (addressing the digital divide) without addressing the skills gap leaves citizens unable to use the tools effectively, so both access and training are needed
option_d: Training is unnecessary because digital skills are innate
rationale: The digital skills gap represents the disparity between those who can use digital tools effectively and those who cannot. Providing hardware without training addresses only the digital divide (access) while leaving the skills gap (competence) unresolved.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A school district implements a technology curriculum that assumes all students have home internet access and prior exposure to computers. Which evaluation best assesses this approach?
option_a: This is fair because all students have equal home environments
option_b: This risks exacerbating the digital skills gap because students from disadvantaged backgrounds — affected by income, education, gender, location, and age factors — may lack prior access and fall further behind
option_c: Home environment has no impact on digital skill development
option_d: Prior exposure to computers is universal among students
rationale: The course materials identify income, education, gender, location, and age as factors impacting the digital skills gap. Curricula that assume universal prior access disadvantage students from underrepresented backgrounds who may lack home technology access.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which discipline contributes to HCI by exploring how user perception and environments influence technology interaction?
option_a: The social sciences, including psychology and sociology
option_b: Only computer programming
option_c: Only graphic design
option_d: Only mechanical engineering
rationale: The course materials state that the social sciences contribute to HCI by exploring user perception and environments, with psychology examining cognitive processing and sociology examining social contexts of use.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A design team is evaluating how much mental effort users expend when navigating a complex dashboard. Which HCI component is being assessed?
option_a: Social Context of Use
option_b: Cognitive Load and User Perception
option_c: Accessibility Needs
option_d: Technical Performance
rationale: Cognitive Load and User Perception is the HCI component examining how psychology helps identify how users process information. Assessing mental effort during dashboard navigation directly evaluates cognitive load.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A government website redesign project aims to ensure that citizens with visual impairments, limited English proficiency, and varying educational backgrounds can all complete application forms successfully. Which HCI principle should guide this redesign?
option_a: Only aesthetic minimalism
option_b: Only technical speed optimisation
option_c: Developing inclusive, user-friendly systems that accommodate diverse and marginalised user groups
option_d: Only mobile responsiveness
rationale: The course materials state that HCI is invaluable for developing inclusive, user-friendly systems that accommodate diverse and marginalised user groups. Designing for visual impairments, language barriers, and educational diversity exemplifies this inclusive design principle.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which usability evaluation method involves assessing interface design against established principles or rules of thumb?
option_a: Task-Based Testing
option_b: Interface Comparison
option_c: User Surveys
option_d: Heuristic Evaluations
rationale: Heuristic Evaluations are explicitly defined as assessing interface design against established principles, distinguishing this method from task-based testing and interface comparison.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A company tests two versions of its checkout page — one with a single-page form and one with a multi-step wizard — by having users complete purchases on both and measuring completion rates. Which usability method is being applied?
option_a: Interface Comparison
option_b: Heuristic Evaluation
option_c: Task-Based Testing only
option_d: Accessibility Audit
rationale: Interface Comparison is defined as testing different environments against one another. Comparing two checkout page designs by measuring user performance on each exemplifies this comparative usability method.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A travel booking website discovers that users in Asian markets prefer dense information layouts while users in European markets prefer minimalist designs. Which usability consideration does this finding reflect?
option_a: Only technical functionality
option_b: How cultural norms and language barriers affect different user groups in different environments
option_c: Only colour preferences
option_d: Universal design preferences that apply globally
rationale: The course materials state that usability testing allows developers to understand how cultural norms and language barriers affect different user groups. Regional design preferences directly illustrate the cultural dimension of user experience.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A development team argues that because their software functions correctly, no usability testing is necessary. Which evaluation best assesses this position?
option_a: This is correct because functionality is the only requirement
option_b: Usability testing is always a waste of resources
option_c: This overlooks that software must not only function correctly but also enable users to achieve their goals effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily — which only usability testing can verify
option_d: Users can always figure out how to use functioning software
rationale: Usability is defined as how well a user can interact with a system to achieve specific goals effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily. Correct functionality does not guarantee that users can successfully complete tasks or have a satisfactory experience.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: Which analysis best explains why addressing the digital skills gap requires inclusive pedagogy — teaching strategies designed to support underrepresented learners?
option_a: Underrepresented learners do not need special support
option_b: Inclusive pedagogy is only for advanced students
option_c: Digital skills gaps affect all groups equally
option_d: Because digital skills gaps are influenced by income, education, gender, location, and age, learners from disadvantaged backgrounds need targeted support to overcome barriers that advantaged learners do not face
rationale: The course materials identify income, education, gender, location, and age as factors influencing the digital skills gap, and specify inclusive pedagogy as the response. Targeted support is necessary because disadvantaged learners face compounding barriers that universal approaches may not address.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A community centre wants to help older adults learn to use smartphones for video calls with family. Which approach best addresses the digital skills gap for this specific demographic?
option_a: Adaptive technologies and patient, age-appropriate instruction that accounts for how demographics like age influence engagement with digital tools
option_b: Providing smartphones without any instruction
option_c: Only offering online tutorials
option_d: Assuming older adults cannot learn technology
rationale: The course materials state that demographics including age influence engagement with digital tools, and that software should be designed with adaptive technologies. Age-appropriate instruction with adaptive support directly addresses the skills gap for this demographic.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
stem: A company designs a job application portal that requires high-speed internet, modern browsers, and advanced digital literacy to complete. Which evaluation best assesses the equity implications of this design?
option_a: This is fair because all job seekers have equal digital resources
option_b: This risks excluding disadvantaged groups who may lack high-speed internet, modern devices, or advanced digital skills, thereby perpetuating the digital skills gap and its associated inequalities
option_c: Digital requirements have no impact on job application equity
option_d: All job seekers naturally possess advanced digital literacy
rationale: The digital skills gap is defined as the disparity between those who can use digital tools effectively and those who cannot. Portals with demanding technical requirements exclude those on the disadvantaged side of this gap, reinforcing existing inequalities rather than ameliorating them.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: What is the primary function of a search engine?
option_a: To create websites and online content
option_b: To host websites on the internet
option_c: To allow users to search for information on the internet by entering keywords or phrases
option_d: To provide physical internet connections
rationale: A search engine is explicitly defined as a web-based tool that allows users to search for information on the internet by entering keywords or phrases, retrieving relevant websites, documents, and other resources.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A student types "photosynthesis process in plants" into a tool and receives a list of relevant websites, articles, and images. Which tool is the student using?
option_a: A web browser
option_b: A word processor
option_c: A spreadsheet application
option_d: A search engine
rationale: The student is using a search engine — a web-based tool that retrieves relevant websites, documents, and other resources based on the user's query. The keyword-based search and retrieval of multiple resource types are defining characteristics.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A researcher needs to find academic papers on climate change published in the last five years. Which approach best uses a search engine for this purpose?
option_a: Entering specific keywords and using filters to narrow results to recent academic publications
option_b: Typing a general question without keywords
option_c: Using only social media to find papers
option_d: Avoiding search engines and browsing random websites
rationale: Search engines function by users entering keywords and retrieving relevant digital content. Effective use requires specific query formulation and available filtering tools to narrow results to the desired scope.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A user wants to find video tutorials for learning Python programming. She enters "Python programming video tutorials" into Google and receives relevant YouTube links and educational websites. Which search engine component does this demonstrate?
option_a: Website hosting
option_b: Data Retrieval — the engine retrieving relevant digital content based on the user's query
option_c: Content creation
option_d: Internet infrastructure provision
rationale: Data Retrieval is identified as a key component of search engines, involving the retrieval of relevant digital content based on user queries. The retrieval of video tutorials and educational websites in response to the keyword query exemplifies this function.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: Which analysis best distinguishes the internet from a search engine?
option_a: They are the same thing
option_b: The internet is a search tool while a search engine is the global network
option_c: The internet is the global network of interconnected computers, while a search engine is a tool used to find content within that network
option_d: Search engines existed before the internet
rationale: The course materials clearly delineate the internet (the global network) from search engines (the tool used to find content). This distinction is fundamental to understanding how online information is organised and accessed.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: Which analysis best explains why search engines are essential for navigating the internet effectively?
option_a: The internet contains very few websites, making search unnecessary
option_b: Search engines create all content on the internet
option_c: Without search engines, the internet would not exist
option_d: The internet is a vast, fast global network of interconnected computers, and search engines facilitate finding specific digital content within this vast environment
rationale: The course materials describe the internet as a vast global network and state that search engines facilitate finding specific digital content within this extensive environment. Without search tools, locating relevant information among billions of web pages would be impractical.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A journalist is verifying facts for a news article and needs to distinguish between credible academic sources and unverified blog posts. Which search strategy best leverages the search engine's retrieval function?
option_a: Using advanced search operators to limit results to educational domains and peer-reviewed sources
option_b: Clicking only on the first result regardless of source
option_c: Avoiding search engines and using social media exclusively
option_d: Searching with single generic words
rationale: Search engines retrieve relevant digital content based on user queries. Using advanced operators to filter by domain type (e.g., .edu) helps the retrieval function prioritise credible sources over unverified content.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A student claims that search engines provide completely objective results ranked purely by relevance to the query. Which evaluation best assesses this claim?
option_a: This is correct because search algorithms have no biases
option_b: This is oversimplified because search engine results are influenced by algorithms, personalisation, advertising, and search engine optimisation practices that may affect which results appear prominently
option_c: Search engines always show the most accurate information first
option_d: Search results are identical for every user worldwide
rationale: While search engines retrieve relevant content, the ranking and presentation of results are shaped by complex algorithms, user personalisation, commercial interests, and content optimisation practices that extend beyond pure query relevance.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A teacher wants students to find primary sources for a history project rather than encyclopedia summaries. Which search query approach would best help the search engine retrieve primary source documents?
option_a: Searching for "history" alone
option_b: Searching only on social media
option_c: Using specific terms like "primary source letters World War II archive" to target relevant document repositories
option_d: Searching for "Wikipedia history"
rationale: Search engines retrieve content based on user-entered keywords. Specific, targeted queries that include document-type descriptors ("primary source," "letters," "archive") help the retrieval algorithm locate relevant primary source repositories rather than general summaries.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: Which analysis best explains the relationship between online resources and search engines?
option_a: They are identical concepts
option_b: Online resources are the tools used to find search engines
option_c: Search engines create all online resources
option_d: Online resources are the digital content available on the internet, while search engines are the tools used to locate and access that content
rationale: The course materials clearly delineate online resources (the digital content) from search engines (the tool used to find the content). This three-part distinction — internet (network), online resources (content), search engines (tool) — is fundamental to the module's conceptual framework.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A medical professional needs to find recent peer-reviewed research on a new drug treatment. Which combination of search behaviour and understanding will yield the most reliable results?
option_a: Using a specialised academic search engine, entering precise medical terminology, and critically evaluating the sources retrieved rather than accepting all results uncritically
option_b: Using only general web search and accepting the first result
option_c: Avoiding search engines entirely
option_d: Searching social media for medical advice
rationale: Effective search engine use combines appropriate tool selection (academic search engines), precise query formulation (medical terminology), and critical evaluation of retrieved results. The search engine facilitates finding content, but the user must assess the reliability of what is retrieved.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: Why does a single keyword search often return an overwhelming number of results?
option_a: Because search engines are malfunctioning
option_b: Because the internet contains vast amounts of digital content, and broad queries match many documents across the global network
option_c: Because search engines limit results to the most popular sites
option_d: Because the internet contains very little content
rationale: The course materials describe the internet as a vast, fast global network of interconnected computers. Broad queries match extensive content across this network, necessitating more specific queries to narrow retrieval to relevant results.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: Which analysis best explains why the ability to use search engines effectively is a critical digital skill in modern education and employment?
option_a: Search engines are only used for entertainment
option_b: Effective searching requires no skill
option_c: The vast volume of digital content makes the ability to formulate precise queries, evaluate results, and locate reliable information essential for academic research, professional tasks, and informed decision-making
option_d: All search results are equally reliable
rationale: The course materials describe the internet as vast and search engines as the tools for navigating it. Effective search skills — query formulation, result evaluation, and source assessment — are essential competencies for functioning in an information-rich digital environment.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A student uses a search engine to research a controversial topic and only clicks results that confirm her existing beliefs. Which evaluation best assesses this research approach?
option_a: This is the most efficient way to use a search engine
option_b: Search engines are designed to confirm user beliefs
option_c: This approach guarantees comprehensive understanding
option_d: This undermines the search engine's purpose of facilitating access to diverse digital content, resulting in a biased and incomplete understanding of the topic
rationale: Search engines are designed to facilitate finding specific digital content across the vast internet, including diverse perspectives. Selectively consuming only confirmatory results defeats this purpose and produces an incomplete information base.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A software developer encounters an error message she has never seen before. She copies the error text into a search engine and finds forum discussions and documentation explaining the cause and solution. Which search engine function enabled this problem-solving?
option_a: Data Retrieval — matching the error text query to relevant digital content containing solutions
option_b: Content creation
option_c: Error correction
option_d: Code compilation
rationale: Data Retrieval is the search engine function that matches user queries to relevant digital content. By entering the specific error text, the developer enabled the retrieval algorithm to locate pertinent documentation and discussions.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: Which analysis best explains why search engine results for the same query may differ between users?
option_a: Search engines malfunction randomly
option_b: Personalisation algorithms, location data, search history, and device type all influence which results are prioritised for individual users
option_c: All users see identical results in identical order
option_d: Search engines only show results from one country
rationale: Modern search engines incorporate multiple factors — personalisation, geolocation, search history, and device context — to tailor results. This means the same query may yield different results for different users based on their individual digital profiles.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A librarian is teaching students how to evaluate the reliability of sources found through a search engine. Which criterion is most important for assessing whether a retrieved source is trustworthy?
option_a: Whether it appears first in the search results
option_b: How colourful and attractive the website is
option_c: The authority and credentials of the source creator, the presence of supporting evidence, and whether the content is current and objective
option_d: The length of the web page
rationale: The search engine retrieves content; it does not validate its reliability. Users must critically evaluate retrieved sources based on authority, evidence, currency, and objectivity — skills that complement search engine use.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A student argues that because search engines make information so easy to find, critical thinking and research skills are no longer necessary. Which evaluation best assesses this position?
option_a: This is correct because search engines provide all the analysis needed
option_b: Search engines eliminate the need for source evaluation
option_c: Finding information is identical to understanding it
option_d: This is incorrect because search engines are retrieval tools, not critical analysis tools; the ease of finding information makes the ability to evaluate, synthesise, and apply that information more important, not less
rationale: Search engines facilitate finding digital content but do not analyse, validate, or synthesise it for users. The abundance of easily accessible information increases the importance of critical evaluation and research skills to distinguish reliable from unreliable content.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: Which analysis best explains the three-way distinction between the internet, online resources, and search engines as presented in the course materials?
option_a: The internet is the global network infrastructure; online resources are the digital content hosted on that network; and search engines are the tools that help users locate specific content within the network
option_b: All three terms refer to the same thing
option_c: Online resources are the network, the internet is the content, and search engines are the websites
option_d: Search engines are the network, the internet is the tool, and online resources are the queries
rationale: The course materials clearly delineate these three concepts: the internet (global network), online resources (digital content), and search engines (finding tools). Understanding this distinction is essential for conceptual clarity in digital literacy.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Internet, Online Resources, and Search Engines
stem: A business wants to ensure its website appears when potential customers search for relevant products. Which understanding of search engines should guide their strategy?
option_a: Search engines randomly select websites to display
option_b: Search engines retrieve relevant digital content based on user queries, so the business should ensure its website contains relevant keywords and high-quality content that matches potential customer searches
option_c: Search engines only show paid advertisements
option_d: Search engine visibility is determined solely by website colour schemes
rationale: Search engines function by retrieving relevant digital content based on user-entered keywords. A business seeking visibility must understand this retrieval mechanism and optimise its content to match the queries its potential customers are likely to enter.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: Which of the following is identified as an emerging technology that is reshaping computing and altering how societies function?
option_a: The typewriter
option_b: The postal service
option_c: Artificial Intelligence (AI)
option_d: The mechanical clock
rationale: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is explicitly identified as one of the emerging technologies — alongside Robotics and Quantum Computing — that is reshaping the world of computing and fundamentally altering how societies function.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: A pharmaceutical company uses machine learning algorithms to predict how new molecular compounds will interact with human proteins. Which emerging technology does this application represent?
option_a: Robotics
option_b: Quantum Computing
option_c: Blockchain
option_d: Artificial Intelligence
rationale: Machine learning — a subset of Artificial Intelligence — involves analysing large datasets to extract insights and make predictions. Predicting molecular interactions exemplifies AI's capacity to solve complex computational problems in scientific research.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: A factory installs autonomous robotic arms that can adapt their movements in real-time based on sensor feedback, working alongside human workers without safety cages. Which emerging technology combination does this represent?
option_a: Robotics and Artificial Intelligence
option_b: Only Quantum Computing
option_c: Only traditional automation from the Manual Labour Period
option_d: Only mechanical engineering
rationale: Autonomous robotic arms that adapt in real-time using sensor feedback combine Robotics (physical mechanisation) with Artificial Intelligence (adaptive decision-making). This exceeds traditional automation by incorporating learning and adaptation capabilities.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: A research lab develops a computer that uses quantum bits instead of traditional binary bits, enabling it to solve certain complex problems exponentially faster than conventional supercomputers. Which emerging technology is this?
option_a: Artificial Intelligence
option_b: Quantum Computing
option_c: Advanced Robotics
option_d: Cloud Computing
rationale: Quantum Computing is explicitly identified as one of the three emerging technologies in the course materials. The use of quantum bits (qubits) and exponential speedup for specific problem types are defining characteristics of this technology.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: Which analysis best explains why emerging technologies carry "immense potential" while also introducing "significant ethical dilemmas"?
option_a: They have no potential or ethical concerns
option_b: They are entirely beneficial with no downsides
option_c: AI, robotics, and quantum computing can transform economies and solve complex problems, but they also raise questions about job displacement, privacy, autonomous decision-making, and equitable access to benefits
option_d: Ethical concerns only apply to older technologies
rationale: The course materials explicitly state that emerging technologies carry immense potential to transform economies, improve quality of life, and solve complex problems while introducing significant ethical dilemmas. This dual nature reflects their transformative power and the profound societal choices they present.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: A government proposes banning all AI development to prevent potential job losses. Which evaluation best assesses this policy?
option_a: This is the only effective way to protect employment
option_b: AI has no beneficial applications
option_c: This will have no economic consequences
option_d: This is overly restrictive because AI also carries immense potential to improve quality of life and solve complex problems; a more balanced approach would address ethical concerns through regulation while preserving beneficial innovation
rationale: The course materials identify both the transformative potential and ethical challenges of emerging technologies. An absolute ban eliminates beneficial applications alongside problematic ones, whereas balanced governance can address ethical concerns while preserving innovation.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: A logistics company deploys AI-powered route optimisation that reduces fuel consumption by 20% but eliminates 200 delivery planning jobs. Which analysis best characterises this situation using course concepts?
option_a: Emerging technologies transforming economies while simultaneously raising ethical dilemmas about workforce displacement
option_b: Only negative consequences with no benefits
option_c: Only benefits with no ethical concerns
option_d: A failure of technology
rationale: The situation exemplifies the dual nature described in the course materials: AI transforms economic operations (fuel efficiency gains) while introducing ethical dilemmas (job displacement). This mirrors the framework of immense potential paired with significant societal challenges.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: Which analysis best distinguishes quantum computing from artificial intelligence as emerging technologies?
option_a: They are identical technologies
option_b: Quantum Computing focuses on computational processing using quantum mechanics to solve specific complex problems exponentially faster, while Artificial Intelligence focuses on creating systems that can learn, reason, and make decisions from data
option_c: Quantum Computing is a type of Artificial Intelligence
option_d: Neither is an actual emerging technology
rationale: The course materials identify both as distinct emerging technologies. Quantum Computing revolutionises computational capability for specific problem classes, while AI creates adaptive, learning systems. They address different technological domains with different applications.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: A hospital deploys robotic surgical assistants that can perform precise movements beyond human steadiness, while AI systems analyse patient data to recommend treatment options. Which course concept best describes these applications?
option_a: Only the Digital Divide
option_b: Only Hardware Obsolescence
option_c: Emerging Technologies (Robotics and AI) reshaping how societies function by transforming healthcare quality of life and problem-solving capabilities
option_d: Only E-waste Management
rationale: The scenario demonstrates both Robotics (surgical precision) and AI (treatment recommendations) — two of the three emerging technologies identified in the course materials — transforming healthcare delivery and exemplifying how these technologies alter societal functioning.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: A corporation develops an AI system that can predict employee burnout with 95% accuracy but requires monitoring all employee digital communications. Which evaluation best assesses this application?
option_a: This is entirely beneficial because it protects employee health
option_b: Privacy concerns are irrelevant in workplace settings
option_c: The accuracy makes ethical concerns unimportant
option_d: While the potential to improve quality of life is significant, the ethical dilemmas around surveillance, privacy, and consent must be carefully weighed against the benefits
rationale: The course materials state that emerging technologies carry immense potential while introducing significant ethical dilemmas. High accuracy does not eliminate the need to address privacy, surveillance, and consent concerns that such monitoring systems raise.

course_name: SSC 202 — Introduction to Computer and Its Application
module_name: Topic 9: Emerging Technologies and Future of Computing
stem: Which three technologies are identified in the course materials as reshaping the world of computing?
option_a: Artificial Intelligence (AI), Robotics, and Quantum Computing
option_b: Typewriters, Fax machines, and Landline telephones
option_c: CDs, DVDs, and Floppy disks
option_d: Steam engines, Water wheels, and Windmills
